title: Dog Nail Growing into Paw Pad: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: Learn how to handle a dog nail growing into the paw pad, the urgency of the situation, and why a veterinary consultation is necessary for treatment.
content_html:
What Is an Ingrown Dog Nail?

An ingrown nail occurs when a dog's claw, most commonly the dewclaw, grows long enough to curl back and penetrate the sensitive skin of the paw pad. This condition causes direct trauma to the tissue and creates an entry point for bacteria.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

This is considered a medium urgency situation. While it is not typically a life-threatening emergency, it is a very painful condition that requires professional attention. If left untreated, the puncture site can develop a deep-seated infection or abscess. You should aim to have your dog seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.

Why Providing a Photo Helps Triage

Sending a clear, well-lit photo of the affected paw to your veterinary team is incredibly helpful. It allows the professional to assess the depth of the nail's penetration and check for clinical signs of infection, such as discharge, extreme swelling, or redness. This information helps them determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and whether they should prepare for a surgical extraction or a standard trim.

faqs.question: Can I trim the ingrown nail at home?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to trim an embedded nail at home. Because the nail has pierced the skin, cutting it can be extremely painful, and your dog may react defensively. Additionally, the embedded portion of the nail may need to be removed under professional supervision to ensure no fragments remain.

faqs.question: What are the signs that the paw pad is infected?
faqs.answer: Signs of infection include a foul odor, yellow or bloody discharge, significant swelling of the toe, and the area feeling hot to the touch. Your dog may also limp or obsessively lick the paw.

faqs.question: How can I prevent this from happening again?
faqs.answer: The best prevention is regular nail maintenance. Check your dog's nails every 2 to 4 weeks, paying special attention to the dewclaws (the thumbs located higher up on the leg), as these do not touch the ground and do not wear down naturally.
